informative.title: Time-domain heart rate variability metrics
informative.summary: Computes standard time-domain HRV metrics from normal-to-normal intervals.
informative.description: The input contains normal-to-normal intervals. Abnormal intervals include intervals affected by ectopic beats, missed detections, false detections, and other artifacts. Correction or removal of abnormal intervals occurs before calling this algorithm.
notes: Unresolved behavior: input containing only NaN values.
Unresolved behavior: one valid NN interval.
Unresolved behavior: no valid successive differences.
Unresolved behavior: only one valid successive difference when sample standard deviation is requested.

references.note: Supports the conventional definitions and units of the time-domain HRV metrics.

definitions.text: A valid NN interval is finite, positive, expressed in seconds, and not NaN.
Mean heart rate is 60 / mean(valid nn_intervals), where the mean is calculated over valid NN intervals.
SDNN is the sample standard deviation of valid NN intervals using denominator N - 1, converted from seconds to milliseconds.

Successive differences are calculated between adjacent entries in the original ordered NN interval vector. NaN values are not removed before calculating differences. A difference is valid only when both adjacent intervals are valid; [1.0, NaN, 1.1] does not produce a difference between 1.0 and 1.1.
RMSSD is the square root of the mean squared valid successive differences, converted from seconds to milliseconds.
SDSD is the sample standard deviation of valid successive differences using denominator N - 1, converted from seconds to milliseconds.
pNN50 is 100 * count(abs(valid successive differences) > 0.05 s) / number of valid successive differences. The threshold is strictly greater than 50 ms.

behavior.nan_handling: NaN intervals are excluded from interval-based statistics. Differences involving NaN intervals are invalid, and NaN gaps are not bridged.
behavior.input_orientation: Row and column vectors represent the same canonical sequence and must produce scientifically equivalent outputs.
